target.name: Tronocarpine
target.motivation: Tronocarpine has demonstrated significant cytotoxic activity against cells resistant to NF-κB and vincristine. Its potential to reverse multidrug resistance also makes it a promising candidate for therapeutic development.

prior_syntheses.key_strategy: Mn(OAc)3-mediated cyclizations to construct the tetracyclic core
Intermolecular radical oxidative substitution of N-Boc tryptamine for azepino[4,5-b]indolones
Michael/aldol cascade reaction to the pentacyclic core in seven steps
First asymmetric total synthesis (20 steps, 2.2% yield) via Michael/aldol cascade with chiral cinchona-derived phase-transfer catalyst
Updated asymmetric total synthesis (11 steps, 10.8% yield)
Racemic total synthesis (7 steps, 1.87% yield) via one-pot tandem cyclization
Synthesis of (-)-tronocarpine (12 steps, 3.41% yield) via ring-opening functionalization of tertiary amines

success_criteria.free_text: The ultimate goal is the total synthesis of tronocarpine, but the paper focuses on the synthesis of dihydrotronocarpine and the rapid assembly of the pentacyclic system.

key_disconnections.bond_description: double bond of the enone system
key_disconnections.reasoning: To be introduced in the final stage through a dehydrogenation process.
To be obtained by conversion of a nitrile into the methylketone.
To be formed by chemoselective reduction of the corresponding lactam.
Relied on a one-pot Michael/lactamization/Michael cascade process involving tryptamine-derived lactam 9 and Michael double acceptor 10.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The retrosynthetic plan aimed to introduce the enone system late in the synthesis, convert a nitrile to a methylketone, and form the hemiaminal from a lactam. The core pentacyclic system was envisioned to be formed via a Michael/lactamization/Michael cascade from readily available starting materials.

thought.background: The synthesis of tryptamine-derived lactam 9 was previously reported, but with N-Boc protection.
thought.gap: An efficient synthesis of lactam 9 using phthalimide protection is needed.
thought.decision: To synthesize lactam 9, phthalimide-protected tryptamine 14 will be C-2 alkylated with xanthate 15 via an oxidative radical addition, followed by phthalimide removal and concomitant lactamization.

outcome.observation: Lactam 9 was generated in 40% overall yield in two steps from phthalimide-protected tryptamine 14 and xanthate 15.

evidence.text_span: Thus, the synthesis commenced with the synthesis of the tryptamine-derived lactam 9 according to the reported methodology with a slight modification.5c Phthalimideprotected tryptamine 14 (instead of the reported N-Boc protected)5,6 was C-2 alkylated with the xanthate 15 via an oxidative radical addition. Removing the phthalimide group with concomitant lactamization generated lactam 9 in 40% overall yield in two steps (Scheme 2).

thought.background: The Michael double acceptor 10 is a key starting material for the cascade reaction.
thought.gap: An efficient synthesis of Michael double acceptor 10 is required.
thought.decision: To synthesize 10, methyl 2-(bromomethyl)acrylate 17 will be obtained via Baylis-Hillman reaction of methyl acrylate and formaldehyde, followed by bromination. A second Baylis-Hillman reaction with acrylonitrile will then generate 10.

outcome.observation: Michael double acceptor 10 was synthesized with an overall yield of 54% in three steps.

evidence.text_span: On the other hand, synthesizing the double Michael acceptor 10 involved obtaining methyl 2-(bromomethyl)acrylate 17 through a Baylis−Hillman reaction using methyl acrylate and formaldehyde, followed by bromination. A second Baylis−Hillman reaction with acrylonitrile then generated the desired product 10 with an overall yield of 54% in the three steps (Scheme 2).14

thought.background: Lactam 9 and Michael double acceptor 10 are available. The proposed mechanism involves deprotonation of lactam 9, first 1,4-Michael addition, lactamization, second carbanion formation, and intramolecular 1,4-Michael addition.
thought.gap: Optimal conditions for the Michael/lactamization/Michael cascade process need to be identified.
thought.decision: To initiate the cascade, lactam 9 and acceptor 10 will be combined in acetonitrile with benzyltriethylammonium chloride (TEBA) as PTC and potassium carbonate as base.

outcome.observation: Compound 13 was obtained in trace amounts. Low solubility of lactam 9 was observed, likely contributing to the low yield.

evidence.text_span: Thus, in the first experiment, lactam 9 and the double Michael acceptor 10 were combined in acetonitrile using benzyltriethylammonium chloride (TEBA) as a phase-transfer catalyst (PTC) and potassium carbonate as the base. However, after 17 h under these conditions, compound 13 was obtained in trace amounts (Table 1, entry 1). The low solubility of lactam 9, which was observed, might likely contribute to the low yield.

thought.background: The previous attempt in acetonitrile with TEBA and K2CO3 resulted in trace amounts of product, possibly due to low solubility of lactam 9.
thought.gap: A more suitable solvent and conditions are needed to improve the yield of the cascade reaction.
thought.decision: To improve the outcome, the reaction will be carried out in DMF, a solvent with a high dielectric constant, at 82 °C.

outcome.observation: The yield increased to 18% when using DMF at 82 °C.

evidence.text_span: We evaluated various solvents, temperatures, phasetransfer agents, and bases to improve the outcome. Thus, when the reaction was carried out using a solvent with a high dielectric constant, such as dimethylformamide (DMF), at 82 °C, the yield increased to 18% (entry 2).

thought.background: DMF improved the yield to 18%. Other solvents might be more effective.
thought.gap: Determine if DMSO offers further improvement for the cascade reaction.
thought.decision: To evaluate solvent effects, the reaction will be conducted in DMSO.

outcome.observation: Using DMSO did not improve the outcome, yielding only traces of product.

evidence.text_span: However, using dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) did not improve the outcome (entry 3).

thought.background: DMF at 82 °C gave 18% yield. Room temperature reactions showed no conversion.
thought.gap: Evaluate alternative phase-transfer agents and bases.
thought.decision: To explore different catalysts, TEBA will be replaced with 1.5 equiv of benzyltrimethylammonium hydroxide (Triton B) and 9 equiv of potassium carbonate in DMF.

outcome.observation: Replacing TEBA with Triton B and K2CO3 resulted in a yield of 13%, which is lower than the previous best result.

evidence.text_span: On the other hand, replacing the phase-transfer agent with 1.5 equiv of benzyltrimethylammonium hydroxide (Triton B) and 9 equiv of potassium carbonate resulted in a yield of 13% (entry 4).

thought.background: Triton B is known to function as both a phase-transfer agent and a base. The previous attempt with Triton B and K2CO3 gave 13% yield.
thought.gap: Investigate the role of Triton B as a base in the absence of an inorganic base.
thought.decision: To determine if Triton B alone can facilitate the reaction, a test will be conducted using only 2.2 equiv of Triton B for 17 h.

outcome.observation: The yield increased to 26% despite the absence of an inorganic base, indicating Triton B's role as a base.

evidence.text_span: To investigate whether the acid− base reaction had been facilitated by the Triton B or the K2CO3, a test was conducted using only 2.2 equiv of Triton B for 17 h. Under these conditions, the yield was increased to 26% (entry 5) despite the absence of an inorganic base.

thought.background: Triton B alone gave 26% yield. Other strong bases might be more effective.
thought.gap: Evaluate other strong bases for the cascade reaction.
thought.decision: To further improve the yield, NaOH will be tested as the base.

outcome.observation: No reaction was observed when using NaOH.

evidence.text_span: Further attempts to improve the yield by testing bases such as t-BuOK and sodium hydroxide (entries 6 and 7) were not successful.

thought.background: NaOH was unsuccessful. Another strong base, t-BuOK, might be effective.
thought.gap: Evaluate t-BuOK as a base for the cascade reaction.
thought.decision: To test another strong base, t-BuOK will be used in DMSO.

outcome.observation: Using t-BuOK resulted in only trace amounts of product.

evidence.text_span: Further attempts to improve the yield by testing bases such as t-BuOK and sodium hydroxide (entries 6 and 7) were not successful.

thought.background: Triton B alone gave 26% yield. The reaction time and addition method of Triton B might be optimized.
thought.gap: Optimize the addition of Triton B to improve the yield.
thought.decision: To improve the yield, 2.2 equiv of Triton B will be added in five portions at 30 min intervals.

outcome.observation: Adding Triton B in portions resulted in an increased yield of 39%.

evidence.text_span: Next, the reaction time and the equivalents of Michael double acceptor 10 were optimized. In Experiment 8, we added 2.2 equiv of Triton B in five portions at 30 min intervals, which resulted in an increased yield of 39%.

thought.background: Adding Triton B in portions improved the yield to 39%. The amount and addition method of the Michael double acceptor 10 might also be optimized.
thought.gap: Optimize the amount and addition of Michael double acceptor 10.
thought.decision: To further improve the yield, four equivalents of the double acceptor will be added, one per hour.

outcome.observation: The pentacyclic product 13 was isolated in 53% yield under these optimized conditions.

evidence.text_span: The same approach was used to add four equivalents, one per hour, of the double acceptor. Satisfactorily, the pentacyclic product was isolated in 53% yield under these conditions (entry 9).

thought.background: Optimized conditions yielded 53% of product 13. Increasing the amount of Michael double acceptor might further improve the yield.
thought.gap: Determine the effect of increasing the equivalents of Michael double acceptor 10.
thought.decision: To investigate the effect of excess acceptor, the equivalents of the double acceptor will be increased to ten.

outcome.observation: Increasing the equivalents of the double acceptor to ten resulted in a lower yield of 37%.

evidence.text_span: However, increasing the equivalents of the double acceptor to ten resulted in a yield of 37%.

thought.background: Pentacyclic intermediate 13 is available. The next step is the chemo- and diastereoselective reduction of the more electrophilic amide in the N-acylindol fragment to obtain hemiaminal 20a (α-OH).
thought.gap: Identify a reducing agent that selectively produces the α-hydroxyl isomer 20a.
thought.decision: To achieve the desired α-hydroxyl isomer, potassium tri-sec-butylborohydride (K-Selectride) will be used as a bulkier reducing agent.

outcome.observation: K-Selectride favored the formation of the α-hydroxyl isomer 20a (α−OH, 97:3 dr) in 60% yield. This isomer was chosen for further synthesis.

evidence.text_span: Interestingly, using lithium triethylborohydride (Super-Hydride) at −41 °C resulted in the formation of the β-hydroxyl isomer 20b (β-OH), yielding 76%. In contrast, switching to a bulkier reducing agent, potassium tri-sec-butylborohydride (K-Selectride), favored the formation of the α-hydroxyl isomer 20a (α−OH, 97:3 dr) in 60% yield. However, since the natural product contains the α-epimer, synthesis continued using this isomer (Scheme 3).

thought.background: The hemiaminal 20a is available. The next step is to convert the nitrile group into the corresponding methylketone.
thought.gap: Identify a suitable reagent for converting the nitrile to a methylketone.
thought.decision: To convert the nitrile to a methylketone, methylmagnesium bromide will be used.

outcome.observation: No reaction was observed using methylmagnesium bromide.

evidence.text_span: Subsequently, we converted the nitrile group into the corresponding methylketone. In the first attempt, no reaction was observed using methylmagnesium bromide.

thought.background: Methylmagnesium bromide was unsuccessful in converting the nitrile to a methylketone.
thought.gap: A more reactive methylating agent is needed.
thought.decision: To achieve the methylketone, methylmagnesium bromide will be replaced with methyllithium.

outcome.observation: When methyllithium was used, the desired ketone 12 was successfully obtained with a yield of 53%.

evidence.text_span: Satisfactorily, when it was replaced with methyllithium, the desired ketone 12 was successfully obtained, with a yield of 53% (Scheme 3).

thought.background: Methylketone 12 is available. The final step is C-C dehydrogenation to form the enone present in the natural product.
thought.gap: Identify conditions for C-C dehydrogenation to form the enone.
thought.decision: Traditional Saegusa-Ito conditions will be initially proposed to perform this transformation.

outcome.observation: Unfortunately, the expected enone was not obtained under these conditions. Increasing the temperature to favor the thermodynamic enolate also failed to achieve its formation. Instead, oxidation of the hemiaminal group to form the corresponding amide 21 was observed.

evidence.text_span: As proposed originally, the final reaction step involved C-C dehydrogenation to form the enone present in the natural product. Traditional Saegusa−Ito conditions were initially proposed to perform this transformation, but unfortunately, the expected enone was not obtained under these conditions. Increasing the temperature to favor the thermodynamic enolate also failed to achieve its formation. Conversely, we observed the oxidation of the hemiaminal group to form the corresponding amide 21.

thought.background: Saegusa-Ito conditions failed to produce the enone, leading to oxidation of the hemiaminal to amide 21.
thought.gap: Explore alternative oxidation conditions for α,β-unsaturated carbonyl compounds.
thought.decision: To achieve the enone, the oxidation conditions reported by Nicolaou using IBX will be tested.

outcome.observation: No product formation was observed using the oxidation conditions reported by Nicolaou with IBX.

evidence.text_span: No product formation was observed using the oxidation conditions reported by the group of Nicolaou15 to obtain α,β-unsaturated carbonyl compounds using IBX.

thought.background: IBX conditions were unsuccessful. Other oxidation methods for hemiaminals might be applicable.
thought.gap: Investigate alternative oxidation conditions for the hemiaminal.
thought.decision: To achieve the enone, a primary amine such as benzylamine combined with palladium acetate in DMSO under an oxygen atmosphere will be tested.

outcome.observation: Only oxidation of the hemiaminal to the corresponding amide 21 was observed.

evidence.text_span: Also, when a primary amine such as benzylamine was combined with palladium acetate in DMSO at 75 °C under an oxygen atmosphere, oxidation of the hemiaminal to the corresponding amide 21 was only observed.16

thought.background: Previous attempts at dehydrogenation resulted in unwanted oxidation of the hemiaminal to amide 21. A report by List et al. describes formation of α,β-unsaturated compounds using phosphoric acid and DDQ.
thought.gap: Test the conditions reported by List et al. for forming α,β-unsaturated compounds.
thought.decision: To achieve the enone, compound 12 will be subjected to phosphoric acid and DDQ conditions.

outcome.observation: Unwanted compound 21 (amide) was again the only product observed.

evidence.text_span: Finally, a report by List et al.17 described the formation of α,β- unsaturated compounds using phosphoric acid and DDQ. Unfortunately, when compound 12 was subjected to these conditions, unwanted compound 21 was again the only product observed.

thought.background: All previous attempts at dehydrogenation failed, leading to amide 21. PDC is known for selective oxidation.
thought.gap: Test PDC for selective oxidation of the α-hydroxyl isomer 12.
thought.decision: To achieve the enone, the selective oxidation of α-hydroxyl isomer 12 will be attempted with PDC.

outcome.observation: The selective oxidation of α-hydroxyl isomer 12 was achieved with PDC to afford the amide 21 in 47% yield, but not the desired enone.

evidence.text_span: Additionally, the selective oxidation of α- hydroxyl isomer 12 was achieved with PDC to afford the amide 21 in 47% yield.18 At this stage, we decided not to pursue the synthesis of tronocarpine further.

Overall Yield.interpretation: Overall yield for the synthesis of dihydrotronocarpine from 9 and 10.

Longest Linear Steps.interpretation: Longest linear sequence from lactam 9 and Michael double acceptor 10 to dihydrotronocarpine.

Failed Attempts Count.interpretation: Number of reported failed attempts during the optimization and final steps.

validation.significance: An efficient methodology for constructing the pentacyclic framework of tronocarpine was developed via a Michael/lactamization/Michael cascade reaction. This cascade forms three new bonds and three chiral centers diastereoselectively, directly assembling the complete pentacyclic skeleton. Although the total synthesis of tronocarpine could not be completed due to challenges in the final dehydrogenation step, the method provides a rapid assembly of the core structure and functional groups suitable for transformation into the natural product, paving the way for synthesizing alkaloid analogues.

source.title: Synthesis of Tronocarpine Framework via a Michael/Lactamization/Michael Cascade Process: Synthesis of Dihydrotronocarpine.
source.authors: Castañón-Garcíaa M, Escalantea CH, Mirandaa LD.
